author: somnergy
content:
Formally summarizing that here
https://hackmd.io/@somnergy/witness-trie-validation-zilkworm

author: jsign
content: Cool! Left some questions/comments, but makes sense to me. It will be nice to see what you come up with to include these checks and how much changes your proving performance.

author: somnergy
content: Only comment that would invite further discussions is plain keys being part of the witness (very much in favour, and not to drop it). Having plain keys gives much greater flexibility and dropping it gives no benefit

author: somnergy
content: Of course Geth might not be able to produce plain keys, but that shouldn't be a reason to drop it (i wasn't a part of that discussion)

author: jsign
content: The reason why we didn't include the pre-state keys was because it wasn't used today in any guest program for witness verification. So I think the reasoning was unless we have a clear signal that pre-state keys help in some meaningful way (mainly proving performance), it is better to have a more minimal witness.

author: jsign
content: Since note that adding more inputs means indirectly the guest program should also check those. If guest programs assume the provided pre-state keys are always valid, tehre might be other potential soundness bugs or similar -- so should be considered more carefully. e.g. which effects would have an incomplete list of prekeys, or other accessing something that does not match with what's provided as MPT nodes ,etc. So requires a lot of other tests cases to create

author: jsign
content: Reg Geth, I think they could create them without problem. IIRC, these pre-state keys will be observed during block execution as the witness is being created, so is not related to any potential limitation of ELs not having MPT preimages.

author: somnergy
content: It is used in Zilkworm

author: somnergy
content: There might be potential bugs everywhere for that matter. There is no test today that even validates basic MPT validation, so, clearly a long way to go and the pre-keys are the easiest to verify and that alone gives an advantage, to know the accounts beforehand.

author: somnergy
content: This is good

author: somnergy
content: We may decide to go for a certain design in which we no longer use pre-keys directly. As of today i see no reason to drop it. Witness is already pretty big and dropping pre-keys won't make an order of magnitude difference just a fraction. But as of today, we rely on pre-keys. Reth and Erigon (upcoming) both provide it, and we consume it

author: somnergy
content: Again, it may not be apparent what and why these discussions if we just look at what one guest program. So, it's better to not unilaterally take decisions on the spec. Further the doc above should clarify where I am coming from if it is read fully

author: jsign
content:
Right, but the source wasn't (and isn't?) available at that point so was impossible to know  if someone was relying on it. Just got to know now that's the case in your doc. :) 
In any case, the main argument shouldn't be if someone uses it but more like if it is properly justified from first principles for something concrete like increase proving time performance or similar.

author: jsign
content:
I think any potential bug should be properly covered in tests. Just opened a PR yesterday to start covering for invalid MPT witnesses reg the provided MPT nodes. https://github.com/ethereum/execution-specs/pull/2589  PR description mentions more cases that should be covered IMHO.

There are other tests reg execution witness proper construction, and invalid witness reg `headers` and `bytecodes` [here](https://github.com/ethereum/execution-specs/tree/projects/zkevm/tests/amsterdam/eip8025_optional_proofs).

And yeah, it was (and is) a lot of work... but I think this is necessary to have robust guest programs. If not we might have easy ways for have liveness failures (intentional or unintentional)

author: somnergy
content:
The source that consumes the witness has been available since like October or something. SP1 Hypercube was recently open-sourced.

You just said nobody uses it, i am clarifying that is not true.

Justification: Performance.

author: jsign
content: For the spec writing, we didn't base on any guest program. We thought about things from first principles. And I don't think we have any unilateral approach... we have been sharing progress of all this work for months in the breakout calls and in this channel for getting feedback. We are more than happy for people to help us evolving the specs and tests. That's the main reason why we're chatting now :)

author: jsign
content:
> Justification: Performance.
Do you know how much performance you gain from having and not having these pre-state keys, assuming they are properly checked and not assumed to be correct?
I think that would be a good first data point to start reconsidering this. Would also be nice to get the thoughts of more ELs around this, since if that's the case probably can help everybody

author: somnergy
content:
I don't have the numbers, probably like 10-15%
But yeah, there are many schemes to handle it. ELs don't make guest programs tho.

author: jsign
content: Ethrex and Reth are both working on guest programs

author: jsign
content: Besu is working on them too but they're starting from scratch one in Zig.

author: somnergy
content: Ethrex is very new

author: somnergy
content: Far off

author: somnergy
content: Idk if they are even formally part of the ELs group mentioned, i am not really much into politics of that. Just saying it's very easy to dump a naiive program and claim things and it takes much pain to optimize to the next level
